items.question: What does Gaussian Naive Bayes assume about continuous features?
items.answer: Each feature x_j given class y=k is Normally distributed with its own μ_{k,j} and σ²_{k,j}, and features are conditionally independent given y so P(x|y) factorizes into a product. Independence details are in Conditional Independence Assumption; PDF definition in Common Distributions.

items.question: Write the GNB log-score used to pick a class.
items.answer: score_k = log P(y=k) + Σ_j log N(x_j; μ_{k,j}, σ²_{k,j}). Predict ŷ = argmax_k score_k. Equivalent to maximizing P(y=k) ∏_j N(x_j; μ_{k,j}, σ²_{k,j}) in log form for numerical stability.

items.question: How is GaussianNB trained — what parameters are estimated?
items.answer: Class priors P(y=k) from class counts; per-class per-feature means μ_{k,j} as the sample mean; variances σ²_{k,j} as sample variance (with optional var_smoothing). Closed-form — no gradient descent.

items.question: GNB vs logistic regression — when might GNB win on small data?
items.answer: When features are roughly Gaussian per class and independence is not badly violated, GNB's generative MLE can generalize from few examples because it estimates few parameters per class. Logistic regression makes fewer distributional assumptions but may need more data to set decision boundaries well.

items.question: Why use predict_log_proba instead of multiplying PDFs?
items.answer: Multiplying many Gaussian PDF values causes underflow to zero in floating point. Summing log priors and log PDFs preserves relative class ordering and is the standard stable implementation.
